comments.body: *"Setting the ulimit to 65535" - what is the output of ulimit -n after you've made this change? And what is the output of ulimit -u (limit on processes/threads)?
@Robert The application exits when the last request has finished. But it takes ages to get to that point
@Robert Expect for the imports, its everything. The server is also a basic Spring app with a single endpoint. Nothing special.
What Java version is used to run the program?
@Robert I tried both Java 21 and 25 and the result is the same
If you try the same in other languages do you get the same result?
Did not do the trick, it still hangs at 8K threads
And in the service unit: [Service]    TasksMax=infinity

answers.body:
write a service unit for systemd and put it into system slice (/etc/systemd/system).
as long as you execute it under a user (even root), you will be restricted.

I'm sorry, I cannot reproduce your problem: But still my Java application can't open more than 8K outgoing connections.

I can start 10,000 connections for testing.

Server Side

DemoApplication.java

  • default
package com.example;

import org.springframework.boot.SpringApplication;
import org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.SpringBootApplication;

@SpringBootApplication
public class DemoApplication {

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SpringApplication.run(DemoApplication.class, args);
    }

}

HelloController.java

  • Test the connection and record the connection in the log file.
package com.example;

import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.GetMapping;
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.PathVariable;
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.RequestParam;
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.RestController;

@RestController
public class HelloController {
    final static Logger logger = LoggerFactory.getLogger(HelloController.class);

    @GetMapping("/hello/{name}")
    public String sayHello(
            @PathVariable String name,
            @RequestParam(value = "greeting", defaultValue = "Hello") String greeting) {

        logger.info("name = {} , greeting = {}", name, greeting);

        return String.format("%s, %s!", greeting, name);
    }
}

application.properties

  • Set the fine-tuning parameters.
spring.application.name=demo
server.shutdown.timeout=30s
server.tomcat.threads.max=400
server.tomcat.threads.min-spare=30
server.tomcat.accept-count=500
server.tomcat.max-connections=12000

logback-spring.xml

  • Set fine-tuning parameters and use them to record each connection.
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<configuration>

    <appender name="FILE" class="ch.qos.logback.core.rolling.RollingFileAppender">
        <file>logs/app.log</file>

        <rollingPolicy class="ch.qos.logback.core.rolling.SizeAndTimeBasedRollingPolicy">
            <fileNamePattern>logs/app-%d{yyyy-MM-dd}.%i.log.zip</fileNamePattern>
            <maxFileSize>10MB</maxFileSize>
            <maxHistory>30</maxHistory>
            <totalSizeCap>300MB</totalSizeCap>
        </rollingPolicy>

        <encoder>
            <pattern>%d{yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss.SSS} [%thread] - %msg%n</pattern>
        </encoder>

    </appender>

    <appender name="ASYNC" class="ch.qos.logback.classic.AsyncAppender">
        <queueSize>8192</queueSize>
        <discardingThreshold>0</discardingThreshold>
        <neverBlock>false</neverBlock>
        <appender-ref ref="FILE"/>
    </appender>

    <root level="INFO">
        <appender-ref ref="ASYNC"/>
    </root>

</configuration>

Build

mvn clean package

java -jar target/app.jar

Here you can see that using hey can successfully test 10,000 connections. This initially rules out a limitation caused by system settings.

Check the app.log file in the logs directory; you'll see 10001 records. Confirm that each record has been entered into the log.

Shut down the Spring Boot application and delete the logs/app.log file.

SimpleRunner.java

  • I made minor modifications to some of the code.
package com.example;

import java.io.IOException;
import java.net.URI;
import java.net.URL;
import java.net.http.HttpClient;
import java.net.http.HttpRequest;
import java.net.http.HttpResponse;
import java.time.Duration;
import java.util.concurrent.ExecutorService;
import java.util.concurrent.Executors;
import java.util.stream.IntStream;

public class SimpleRunner {
    HttpClient client = HttpClient.newBuilder()
            .version(HttpClient.Version.HTTP_1_1)
            .followRedirects(HttpClient.Redirect.NORMAL)
            .connectTimeout(Duration.ofDays(1))
            .build();

    public static void main(String... args) throws IOException {
        long pid = ProcessHandle.current().pid();
        System.out.println("pid = " + pid);
        SimpleRunner runner = new SimpleRunner();
        try (var executor = Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor()) {
            runner.benchmarkingMethod(executor);
        }
    }

    private void benchmarkingMethod(ExecutorService executor) {
        IntStream.range(0, 10_000).forEach(i ->
                executor.submit(() -> {
                    try {
                        String response1 =  fetchJavaNineStyle(
                                URI.create("http://localhost:8080/hello/AAAA").toURL()
                        );
                    } catch (Exception e) {
                        throw new RuntimeException(e);
                    }
                })
        );
    }

    String fetchJavaNineStyle(URL url){
        try {
            return client.sendAsync(HttpRequest.newBuilder().GET().uri(url.toURI()).build(), HttpResponse.BodyHandlers.ofString()).get().body();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            throw new RuntimeException(e);
        }
    }
}

Build

mvn clean package

Run

before run SimpleRunner , start spring boot application again.(Before starting Spring Boot Application, please ensure that the logs/app.log file has been deleted.)

In Spring Boot Application Directory, open terminal.

terminal-1:

java -jar target/app.jar

open another terminal.

terminal-2:

mkdir -p t-logs

COUNTER=1
MAX_COUNT=100
CURRENT_DATE=$(date +%Y%m%d)
while true; do
    CURRENT_TIME=$(date +%H%M%S)
    FORMATTED_COUNTER=$(printf '%03d' $COUNTER)
    SERIAL_NUMBER="${CURRENT_DATE}_${CURRENT_TIME}_${FORMATTED_COUNTER}"
    ss -tanp | grep 8080 | grep ESTAB  | wc -l >  t-logs/${SERIAL_NUMBER}.log
    ss -tanp | grep 8080 | grep ESTAB >>  t-logs/${SERIAL_NUMBER}.log
    sleep 1
    COUNTER=$((COUNTER + 1))
done

In SimpleRunner Directory, Open terminal:

  • The time instruction is added before Java instructions to record execution time.

terminal-3:

time java -Dfile.encoding=UTF-8 -Dsun.stdout.encoding=UTF-8 -Dsun.stderr.encoding=UTF-8 -cp "target/classes" com.example.SimpleRunner

If SimpleRunner completes execution, go to terminal 2 and press Ctrl + C.

Result:

$ time java -Dfile.encoding=UTF-8 -Dsun.stdout.encoding=UTF-8 -Dsun.stderr.encoding=UTF-8 -cp "target/classes" com.example.SimpleRunner
pid = 14870

real    0m5.522s
user    0m16.611s
sys 0m2.194s

You can check the logs/app.log file in your Spring Boot application to see if there are 10,000 records. If so, confirm that each request had a successful connection and was recorded.

...
2025-11-30 11:58:46.291 [http-nio-8080-exec-231] - name = AAAA , greeting = Hello
2025-11-30 11:58:46.292 [http-nio-8080-exec-231] - name = AAAA , greeting = Hello
2025-11-30 11:58:46.292 [http-nio-8080-exec-231] - name = AAAA , greeting = Hello
2025-11-30 11:58:46.296 [http-nio-8080-exec-373] - name = AAAA , greeting = Hello

Note that I did not switch to the root account or use sudo to execute it. I executed it under my regular user account.

How do we verify that the client is genuinely establishing 10,000 concurrent connections?

  • Navigate to the t-logs directory.

  • Identify the file with the largest size. For example, the filename might be 20251130_115845_006.log.

  • Examine the contents: (or View the contents:)

20000
ESTAB  0      0      [::ffff:127.0.0.1]:8080  [::ffff:127.0.0.1]:42168 users:(("java",pid=14754,fd=2516)) 
ESTAB  0      0      [::ffff:127.0.0.1]:8080  [::ffff:127.0.0.1]:52152 users:(("java",pid=14754,fd=485))  
ESTAB  0      0      [::ffff:127.0.0.1]:8080  [::ffff:127.0.0.1]:42030 users:(("java",pid=14754,fd=2499)) 
ESTAB  0      0      [::ffff:127.0.0.1]:56290 [::ffff:127.0.0.1]:8080  users:(("java",pid=14870,fd=7148)) 
ESTAB  0      0      [::ffff:127.0.0.1]:32998 [::ffff:127.0.0.1]:8080  users:(("java",pid=14870,fd=1528)) 
ESTAB  0      0      [::ffff:127.0.0.1]:8080  [::ffff:127.0.0.1]:36710 users
  • The first line, 20,000, indicates that there are 20,000 connections associated with port 8080, as confirmed by the command: ss -tanp | grep 8080 | grep ESTAB | wc -l

Why are there 20,000 connections? The Client-side SimpleRunner utilizes 10,000 connections, and the Server-side Spring Boot uses 10,000 connections.

Next, we examine pid=14870, which belongs to the Client-side SimpleRunner. We can also use the following command to check the number of connections for this specific process: cat 20251130_115845_006.log | grep "pid=14870" | wc -l

$ cat 20251130_115845_006.log | grep "pid=14870" | wc -l

10000

We can confirm that the number of connections is indeed 10,000.

In addition, my system's ulimit -u settings are as follows:

$ ulimit -n
1024

$ ulimit -u
127572

title: Cant open more than 8000 tcp connections with Java
body:
I am trying to open to open 10K+ connections to a server, but my app stops at 8K connections when looking at the server logs. When using two remote servers it will struggles when reaching between 8K and 9K connections. Making me belief the problem is on the client side.

I tried the following settings on the client:

  • Setting the ephemeral ports:net.ipv4.ip_local_port_range = 32768 60999

  • Setting the ulimit to 65535

But still my Java application can't open more than 8K outgoing connections.

Some experimenting showed that:

  • Doing a little less than 8K in rapid succession is fine.

  • Doing 8.1K requests causes a 30 delay before it continues with only little bursts.

  • During the tests the machine never runs out of CPU or memory. During the delay it goes back to running at an idle 1-2%.

  • No error or exception is being thrown.

The code:

import java.io.IOException;
import java.net.URI;
import java.net.URL;
import java.net.http.HttpClient;
import java.net.http.HttpRequest;
import java.net.http.HttpResponse;
import java.time.Duration;
import java.util.concurrent.ExecutorService;
import java.util.concurrent.Executors;
import java.util.stream.IntStream;

public class SimpleRunner {
    HttpClient client = HttpClient.newBuilder()
            .version(HttpClient.Version.HTTP_1_1)
            .followRedirects(HttpClient.Redirect.NORMAL)
            .connectTimeout(Duration.ofDays(1))
            .build();


    public void main(String... args) throws IOException {
        long pid = ProcessHandle.current().pid();
        System.out.println("pid = " + pid);

        try (var executor = Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor()) {
            benchmarkingMethod(executor);
        }
    }


    private void benchmarkingMethod(ExecutorService executor) {
        IntStream.range(0, 10_000).forEach(i ->
                executor.submit(() -> {
                    try {
                        String response1 =  fetchJavaNineStyle(URI.create("http://localhost:8080/v1/delay/0").toURL());
                    } catch (Exception e) {
                        throw new RuntimeException(e);
                    }
                })
        );
    }

    String fetchJavaNineStyle(URL url){
        try {
            return client.sendAsync(HttpRequest.newBuilder().GET().uri(url.toURI()).build(), HttpResponse.BodyHandlers.ofString()).get().body();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            throw new RuntimeException(e);
        }
    }
}
